name: TOI05577.01
comments: USP Observed on 30/09. Report: The transit is too shallow to be detected given the scatter in the light curve. We can rule out 12 as EBs with deep transits. This one is ready to be uploaded to ExoFOP.
comments_sg1: found in faint-star QLP search; The first TRES observation reveals a rather hot star with broad lines. The two TRES observations yield a velocity offset of 1.56 km/s that is out of phase with the photometric ephemeris, but may not be significant due to the very rapid rotation. The correlation peak suggests a composite spectrum, with a fainter star with less rapid rotation. Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a nominal ingress on 20220930 in gp,rp,ip,zs and cleared 12 out of 270 neighbors over the window, did not see an obvious NEB. Check field within 2.5' for NEBs using a red (rp, R, ip, I, z) filter.

name: TOI04741.01
comments_sg1: [P=2.4849639] found in faint-star QLP search; Allyson Bieryla/KeplerCam observed a full (e-262/i+269 min: e-2.3σ/i+2.4σ) on 20220218 in ip and with FWHM detrending, ruled out the 6.8 ppt event using an uncontaminated target aperture. The 7 ppt might still be obscured in the apparent systematics of the undetrended light curve. No obvious NEBs over the window, although several with close neighbors had the same large apparent systematic that is in the beginning of the undetrended T1 light curve. There are no expected ETM1 observations, so if the event doesn't show up on the next try, let's try 4σ late. Remains PC. Update from AIJ-s87. [P=2.4843139] The next observation should be a focused high precision (<1.0 ppt/5 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to determine the source and timing of the event. If the event doesn't show up on the next try, let's try 4σ late.

name: TOI04717.01
comments: Report: We observed full on 20250310 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 2.58 min late (0.2 sigma) (Rp/R*)^2: 9.5, 7.9, 10.4, 6.7 ppt event using 6.96 target aperture contaminated with TIC 757611213
period: T=18.4, dT=5.2, r=2.3". The i-band data after the middle of the egress is somehow absent. The transit depths do not show strong chromaticity. We can rule out all the identified nearby stars."
depth_sg1: [P=4.1583367] found in faint-star QLP search; Ken Mason/ Mason 0.28m/ observed a full on 20250124 in R and detected an ~12 min (0.6σ) late ~13.4 ppt [(Rp/R*)²: 11.4 ppt] event using a contaminated 4 target aperture (T2: TIC 757611213
plan_observations: T=18.4, dT=5.2, r=2.3"). Phil Evans/ El Sauce 0.51m/ observed a full on 20250124 in Rc and detected a ~12 min (0.75σ) late ~11 ppt [(Rp/Rs)^2: 9.8 ppt] event using a contaminated 4.0" target aperture (T2: TIC 757611213, T = 18.4, dT = 5.2, r = 2.3"). [P=4.1583604] The MuSCAT2 team observed a full on 20250310 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time (Rp/R*)^2: 9.5, 7.9, 10.4, 6.7 ppt event using ~7" contaminated with an unresolved nearby star (TIC 757611213, dT = 5.2, r = 2.3"). No more observations needed, except for high spatial resolution (to separate the 2.3" neighbor) full transits in a red (r', R, i', I, z, Y) filter to determine the source of the event. Multi-band or blue (U, u', B, g') filter observations even better for a simultaneous chromaticity check."

name: TOI05579.01
comments: Observed on 28 January 2024, Report: We observed a full on 20240128 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time (Rp/R*)^2: 38.1, 38.0, 38.3, 36.8 ppt event on a near-grazing orbit (b~0.8~1-Rp/Rs), using a 10.9 uncontaminated target aperture. The radius ratios are consistent i.e. achromatic within 1 sigma. We can rule out deep eclipses in all the identified nearby stars. Observed on 19 December 2023, Report: We observed full on 20231219 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 1.0 min late (1.2 sigma) (Rp/R*)^2: 60.0, 34.4, 37.5, 34.5 ppt event using 7.395" uncontaminated target aperture. The transit depths do not show strong chromaticity except for gp, although the significance is low because the data is obtained until the start of the egress and scattered due to FOV jumps. We can rule out all the identified nearby stars."
comments_sg1: [P=19.8628449] found in faint-star QLP search; Update to AIJ multi-year [P=19.8735449] GRoss, AVand, ZdBeurs analyzed ZTF data and detected an on-target 40 ppt event using an uncontaminated 2.5 target aperture and a revised period of 19.87276689 d. Rick Schwarz/ Khalid Barkaoui/ LCO-HAL-2m0M3 analyzed a full on 20231021 in MUSCAT3 gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 26 min (1.7σ) early ~28.0 ppt [(Rp/R*)²: 19.2, 20.9, 20.5, & 21.4 ppt] event using an uncontaminated 3.5" target aperture. [P=19.8729909]. Greg Srdoc/ Kotizarovci-0.3m analyzed a full on 20240128 in ~T and detected an ~on time ~37.1 ppt [ (Rp/R*)²: 30.3 ppt] event using an uncontaminated 4.8" target aperture. JeromeL/ MuSCAT2 team/ observed a full on 20240128 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time (Rp/R*)^2: 38.1, 38.0, 38.3, 36.8 ppt event using an uncontaminated 10.9" target aperture. No more SG1 observations needed at this time."

name: TOI03821.01
comments: Observed on Jan 6, 2025. There are significant residuals that may be due to stellar variability as reported in other previous SG1 observations. We can rule out deep eclipses in all the other identified nearby stars."
comments_sg1: [P=2.596103] found in faint-star QLP search; 10 ppt stellar variability. Allyson Bieryla/KeplerCam observed a full (e-307/i+202 min: e-1.0σ/i+0.7σ) on 20211118 in ip and ruled out the 9.7 ppt event on target (although the target seems to show short-term +/- 10 ppt variability) with an uncontaminated aperture and cleared 41/41 neighbors over the window. We'll get an ephem update from s47 data. In the meantime we can try 2σ late. [P=2.597765] Allyson Bieryla/KeplerCam observed a full on 20220326 in ip and detected a variable light curve on the target using an uncontaminated 4.0 target aperture. AIJ s20+s47 FFI analysis gives P = 2.597265 +/- 0.00002 d, and shows the event happened 1.4σ early relative to the original ephemeris. [P=2.597265] Allyson Bieryla/KeplerCam observed an ingress (e-168/i+13 min: e-33.7σ/i+2.5σ) on 20230422 in ip and was unable to detect the 9.2 ppt event due to a variable target star using an uncontaminated aperture but cleared 38/38 neighbors over the window. SPOC is consistent with on-target. Paul Benni/ASP observed a full (e-260/i+260 min
depth_sg1: -43σ/+43σ) on 20231222 in rp and maybe detecetd a ~7.7 ppt event using uncontaminated 6.0" target apertures, however, the variable star makes the detection challenging. LJerome de Leon/ MuSCAT2 team/ observed a full on 20250106 in gp, rp, ip, zs and apparently detected an ~on-time [(Rp/R*)^2: 10.4, 10.6, 12.0, 11.7 ppt] event using an uncontaminated 10.9" target aperture. The strong ~10 ppt stellar variability complicates the interpretation. Fabian Rodriguez Frustaglia/RFAC observed a nominal full (e-253 min/i+182min
plan_observations: -19/14σ) on 20250114 in Red and possibly detected a ~4 ppt event using an uncontaminated 7.3" target aperture. The stellar variability makes interpreting the lightcurve challenging. On hold awaiting statistical validation or RV confirmation. No more SG1 observations needed at this time. We will likely never detect this one from the ground due to the need for long base-line."

depth_sg1: found in faint-star QLP search; The two TRES observations yield a velocity offset of 926 m/s that is in phase with the photometric ephemeris and is consistent with a companion with a mass of a few Jupiters. The strong sodium D lines have structure, which suggest that this may be a distant and therefore luminous star. The broad lines will make precise velocities very challenging. Confirm event on target with high precision (<~1.5ppt/min) and/or check field for NEBs within 2.5' using a red (rp, R, ip, I, z) filter.

name: TOI05082.01
comments: Observed on 7th of February 2024 Report: The observations were affected by clouds. The telescope was defocused to avoid saturation of the target ( V = 8.2 mag). We do not detect the transit event on target, the light curve presents a large scatter and the predicted transit depth is 0.9 mmag. We can rule out 1 star as an EB with deep transits during the observed window. ================

comments: CARMENES target, TTV target (Akihiko) Submitted to TFOP on 7/1/2021 Report: Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a nominal full (i-21 min/e+161 min) on 20211229 in gp,rp,ip,zs and ruled out the 1.847 ppt event on target and cleared out 14 out of 189 neighbors over the window. I think the data are useless. Observed on 16th of February 2024 Report: We detect the egress on target.
comments_sg1: [P=6.222236] This one has some history. SPOC has a 4.7 sigma odd-even difference. The first transit (n=0) is deeper. The 2nd and 4th (n=1 and 3) transits are shallower. The third (n=2) transit falls in a data gap. The vetting note suggests this could be two different planets, one at 12.4 days and the other at longer period. It could also be an EB primary and secondary; JohnK observed an (odd/n=11) ingress -45/+15 min on 20200304 in ip and did not see an obvious ingress. Allyson observed a deep ODD (n=11) ingress +/-90 min on 20200304 in ip and cleared the field over +/-1.5σ, although T3 (18 WSW) shows a suspicious downward trend. Pablo Lewin observed a (odd/n=11) full on 20200304 in Lewin Ic and possibly detected a ~15 minute early roughly 3 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 6.3" target aperture, but a large shift in x,y coincides with a jump in the baseline that likely caused the apparent ingress signal. [P=6.221453] RickS analyzed a defocused (odd/n=19) egress -50/+75 min (-1.5/+2 sigma) on 20200423 in zs and did not detect an egress in the undetrended data, but maybe detected in double-detrended data. Ryan Cloutier analyzed an ODD (n=47) (SPOC-based even transit) defocused egress -50/+140 min (-0.6/+1.8σ) on 20201014 in 1m0 zs and detected a ~15 min late 1.5-2 ppt (depending on comp ensemble) in a 7.8" uncontaminated target aperture. The field is cleared over -0.6/+1.8σ. Rick Schwarz analyzed a full (Tc -212/+101 min
depth_sg1: -2.5/+1.2 sigma) of overlapping .01 and .02 on 20201114 in 1m0 zs and detected a ~60 min early (0.6 sigma) 0.7 ppt transit (apparently .02), and a possible ~30 min late ~2 ppt egress (possibly .01). However, the ingress of .01 is not distinct, but could be obscured by the multiple signals and trends. Rick Schwarz analyzed a gapped full on 20211104 in 1m0 zs and detected an ~17 min early ~1.5 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 9.8" target aperture. Roberto Zambelli /LCO-MCD-1m0 analyzed an EVEN (n=110) full (e-192/i+305 min
plan_observations: -1.1/+1.7 σ) on 2021110 in zs and ruled out the 1.8 ppt event over the window. This may mean that the first event is not the same as the 2nd and 3rd events. Rick Schwarz/LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed a gapped full on 20211211 in zs and possibly detected a ~31 min (0.16σ) late ~1.7 ppt transit using an uncontaminated 9.8" target aperture. Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a full (i-21 min/e+161 min) on 20211229 in gp,rp,ip,zs and probably ruled out the 1.8 ppt event on target and cleared out 14 out of 189 neighbors over the window. Rick Schwarz/KarenC/LCO-Teid-1m0 analyzed a defocused ingress (+/-0.5σ) on 20211010 in zs and ruled out the 1.8 ppt event on target over the window. AIJ analysis of s20+s47 FFIs give P=6.226171, which is 3σ late, so ingress occurred well-after these observations ended. Update to QLP s47 [P=6.226171] Rick Schwarz/LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed a defocused full on 20230305 in zs and detected an ~on-time ~1.4 ppt event using an uncontaminated 7.8" target aperture. Howie Relles/ LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed a weather-affected full on 20231211 in zs. Inconclusive due to poor skies. Howie Relles/ LCO-TEID-1m0 analyzed a full on 20231204 in zs and detected a ~10 min. early ~1.2 ppt event using an uncontaminated 7.8" target aperture. No more observations needed."

name: TOI04725.01
comments: Observed on 11 November 2024, Report: We restricted the aperture size to avoid flux contamination from the nearby star ID 6 (TIC 26587617, dT = -0.002, r = 3.48). We detect a transit event likely on the target star, there may be some flux contamination from Star ID 6 (companion star is bright)."
comments_sg1: [P=2.0763851] V-shaped; found in faint-star QLP search; Kim McLeod and students/Whitin observed a full on 20220207 in R and detected a 11 min (0.4σ) early 10 ppt event using a contaminated (T2, 3.5, dT=0.0) 5.5" target aperture. Chris Stockdale/Hazelwood observed a full on 20220219 in Rc and probably detected a 30 min (1σ) early ~10 ppt V-shaped event using a contaminated 8.2" target aperture. Chris Stockdale/Hazelwood observed a full on 20220217 in Rc and detected a 26 min (1σ) early 7.5 ppt event using a contaminated 7.7" target aperture. [P=2.0762926] Raquel Forés/Jose Muñoz/OAUV-TURIA2 observed a full on 20230219 in R and apparently ruled out the event on target using a 5.4" aperture that is contaminated with a dT=-0.002 neighbor 3.48" NW, although there is an apparent ~8 ppt egress at about the right time. Maybe systematics affect the ingress??AdamP/SUTO-Otivar-0m3 observed a full on 20221231 in B and detected an ~on-time ~20 ppt transit using a partially contaminated 2" aperture that removes most of the flux of T2. Felipe Murgas/ MuSCAT2 Team/ observed a full on 20241111 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time ~20.8, 23.0, 27.3, 26.7 ppt event using uncontaminated 2.6" target apertures. Mild chromaticity?There is a similarly colored dT=0, r=3.5" neighbor that could be affecting the depth measurements in the bluer bands. ChrisS/ CristilynW/ LCO-SAAO-1m0 analyzed a full on 20250101 in gp and detected an ~on-time, ~10 min (1.7σ) longer duration, ~7.1 ppt event using a contaminated 7.8" target aperture (T2 (dT=0.0, r=3.5" NW). Using a smaller 1.9" aperture, that excludes most of the flux from T2, results in a noisier yet similarly timed, slightly longer duration, and deeper (>20 ppt) event. There was a loss of focus near the end of the observation but those images were removed. Ken Mason/ Mason-0.28m/ observed a full on UT20250201 in R and detected an ~on-time ~6.2 ppt [(Rp/R*)²=5.8 ppt] event using a contaminated 7.8" target aperture (T2: TIC 26587617, dT=0.0, r=3.0" NW). Using a 2.8" aperture that excludes most of the light from T2, T1 shows an ~on-time ~13 minute longer duration (2.5σ), ~17.7 ppt event. On hold awaiting statistical validation or RV confirmation. No more SG1 observations needed at this time."

Update from AIJ-s20+s45. [P=10.7791316] JeromeL/ MuSCAT2 team/ observed a full on 20240127 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a ~51 min early (6.8 sigma) (Rp/R*)^2: 36.8, 30.5, 43.5, 57.1 ppt event using a 10.9" uncontaminated target aperture. The gp band observation is very noisy and the retrieved depth is likely inconclusive. The zs band depth after detrending is nearly ~2x deeper than QLP s45, and this is difficult to interpret as the detrending parameter(s) use are not disclosed. However, comparing the depths in the undetrended rp, ip and zs lightcurves hints at possible chromaticity. AIJ s72 analysis also suggests an earlier event. [P=10.7786547]. The next observation should be a high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to check for the return of the transit at the revised ephemeris. High-precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) multi-band or blue (U, u', B, g') filter observations even better for a simultaneous refinement on the chromaticity check."

depth_sg1: [P=5.7655904] found in faint-star QLP search; Allyson Bieryla/KeplerCam observed a full on 20211206 in ip and detected a 0-6 min (0.7σ) late, possibly 0-30 min longer (depending on egress end interpretation) 9 ppt event on the target using an uncontaminated 3.4 target aperture. Roberto Zambelli/CMO-0.4m observed a full on 20230113 in R and possibly detected an on-time ~9 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 9.5" target aperture. DaveL: An LSD analysis of the first two TRES observations reveals that the spectrum is double-lined. The secondary peak is almost 10 times smaller than the height of the primary peak. The secondary peak swaps sides between quadratures with a reflex motion that is almost ten times larger than the primary peak, which is in the opposite direction, as expected for a double-lined binary. Closer inspection of the primary correlation function confirms the composite nature of the spectrum, with slight bulges that swap sides as expected. The new third TRES observation continues the pattern, although we don't have an LSD analysis for it yet. The secondary is luminous, so it is hard to see how it could be a planet. A TODCOR analysis might be worth the effort to better characterize the faint companion. Jerome de Leon/ MuSCAT2 observed a full on 20231028 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 6 min early (Rp/R*)^2: 8.5, 10.4, 9.6, 10.3 ppt event using an uncontaminated 10.9" target aperture. [P=5.7655534]. The next observation should be a high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to check for the return of the transit at the revised ephemeris."

name: TOI06940.01
comments: Resumed in Dec 2025, Toshi's target: the goal is to determine its precise radius. 3 additional transits are desired. Although high priority, it can be treated as priority 1.5, as there are many transit opportunities. Observed on 25th of March. 2024 Report: We do not detect the transit event with significance, the predicted transit depth is too shallow. We can rule out 63 stars as EBs with deep transits during the observed window. This is an M star (Teff = 3338K, V = 15 mag), candidate is a USP with P = 0.305 days, predicted Rp ~ 2.0 Rearth. The 10 min. binned light curve kind of shows the transit but this also happened at the same time of a small jump in the guiding (Y-axis) so it could be a caused by a poor model fit. observed on 1st of April 2024 Report: We do not detect the transit event with significance. There could be a hint of a transit in the i- an zs-band light curves and in the 10 min. binned light curve. We rule out 63 stars as EBs with deep transits in the observed window. Report 20/12/2025: We do not detect the transit event with significance. The transit is likely visible on the 10 min. binned light curve. Report 22/12/2025: We do not detect the transit event with significance. The transit depths seem to be at odds with the results of the 20/12/2025 and 30/12/2025 observations. Report 30/12/2025: We do not detect the transit event with significance. The transit is likely visible on the 10 min. binned light curve.

depth_sg1: found in faint-star QLP search; possibly on TIC 280307607; The velocities from the four TRES observations show a general drift down over the past month and do not follow the photometric ephemeris. Furthermore, all three recent exposures show a bulge on the red side of the correlation peak, whereas the first exposure does not. This TOI has a composite spectrum with a slow widening of the velocity separation. It could be a longish-period spectroscopic binary with an inner planet around either star. It will be difficult to dig out motion at a four-day period for either component, and the planet radius will be roughly twice as large due to dilution. Jerome de Leon/ MuSCAT2 observed a full on 20250228 in gp, rp, ip, zs and probably detected a 1.3 ppt event using a 10.9 target aperture contaminated with a nearby star (TIC 280307607, Tmag=12, dT=0.9, r=6.14"). We can rule out deep eclipses in most of the identified nearby stars. The next observation should be a focused high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to determine the source of the event (need to separate the 6" neighbor)."

name: TOI06935.01
comments: Tianjun Gan Program: Giant planet candidate around an M star, it is important to get good S/N in g and r bands. 6935.01 on 10 November. 2024 Report: The observations were affected by dome vignetting during the length of the data acquisition. We detect a transit event on the target star, because of the dome vignetting we can not check for any color-dependancy on the transit depth. The data is only useful for the ephemeris. I attached screenshots of the 'saw' pattern of star-fluxes caused by the dome rotating (see screenshot 1); when the star reached its highest elevation in the sky there was a flux jump (see screenshot 2). I used the full time series for the transit fit, using only the first part before the 'flux jump' we still detect the ingress. Observed on 3 December 2024 Report: We restricted the aperture size to avoid flux contamination from nearby stars. We detect a deep transit event on the target star.

[P=1.82377] Pere Guerra observed a full with a short cloud break on 20200205 in OAA Ic and possibly detected a 39 minute late 2.5 ppt event on target (relative to orign ephem). Rick Schwarz observed a full (with short pre-OOT) on 20200215 in alternating 1m0 B and zs. Ryan Cloutier observed a full on 20200227 in B and confirmed a ~3 ppt transit on target. I agree with Ryan that I'm not convinced the depth is deeper than TESS and zs. KarenC and EricJ analyzed a full on 20210108 in MuSCAT3 gp,rp,ip,zs and detected 1.3,1.8,1.4,1.6 ppt transits in uncontaminated 4" apertures. EricJ's analysis shows: a wide range of dilution factors are allowed by the data, with no strong preference for a chromatic solution. [P=1.8237571] No strong chromaticity. no more observations needed at this time."

name: TOI01012.01
comments: EPHEMERIS ERROR: 20 min Confirmed by SG1 as NEB and retired QLP S7 PC: Confirm event on target with high precision (<~1.5ppt/min) and/or check field for NEBs within 2.5' using a red (rp, R, ip, I, z) filter. Submitted TFOP 31/10/19
comments_sg1: DWL: The second TRES observation shows a modest velocity shift for such a rapidly rotating star, and it is out of phase with the photometric ephemeris. This pretty much rules out a brown dwarf companion. Felipe and the MuSCAT2 team observed a full on 20191028 in gp,rp,ip,zs and confirmed a possible chromatic transit in an 11 target star aperture that is not contaminated with a star bright enough to cause the TESS detection. The main concern from my perspective is that the rp, ip, and zs depths are about 2x the TESS-band depth, while the gp depth is similar to the TESS-band depth. QLP s07 shows odd/even sigma=1.7, SPOC s34 shows an odd/even sigma=2.3, but then when the two sectors are combined in QLP s34, the odd/even sigma becomes 11.9 (in all 3 cases, the odd signals are deeper). The huge increase with just two sectors combined sounds a little bit suspicious to me, but the event is V-shaped and it's not an L1 candidate, so I'll let it go for now as an EB. Retired as APC/EB?."

name: TOI01709.01
comments: EPHEMERIS ERROR: 6 min Submitted to TFOP 12/ March/ 2021 Report: The target star has a faint companion nearby (star ID 11) that needs to be checked if it is the source of the transit. With that in mind we ran two analysis: one without limiting the aperture sizes and another using just the two smallest apertures (files ending with 'SmallAper'). With the full aperture range we detect the ingress on target but it is difficult to say if star ID 11 is causing the transit (as expected). By limiting the aperture size we detect the ingress on target; for the 'possible blends' plot we set as aperture size the smallest aperture possible to avoid flux contamination on target and star ID 11 as much as possible. Looking at the light curve plot for star ID 11 using the smallest aperture possible we can rule out this star as the one causing the transit, since the ingress is not visible and according to the plot it should be deeper than the scatter we see in the light curve. Tc: 2459284.4151284 ± 0.0029073 T14: 0.13493 ± 0.00485 days
comments_sg1: period could be 2x (now confirmed to be 1x); Didier Laloum observed an (odd) full on 20200324 in R and detected a ~6-7 ppt ~on-time event in a target aperture that is contaminated with a dT = 4.2 neighbor 6 SW. Trending chromatic. Tomasz Popajewski observed a full on 20200324 in clear and detected an 8 ppt transit that is ~30 short in duration. The 15" aperture is contaminated with a dT = 4.2 neighbor 6" SW. RobertoZ observed a full on 20200416 in clear and detected an on-time 8 ppt transit in the contmaianted target aperture. Chuck Cynamon observed a full on 20210124 in R and could not separate T1/T2. John Kielkopf observed a full on 20210303 in rp and confirmed a 10 ppt event on target. Felipe and the MuSCAT2 team observed an ingress on 20210310 in gp,rp,ip,zs and detected an ~on-time likely achromatic ~10 ppt ingress in the T1/T2 pair. In a smaller aperture (size not provided), an ~8 ppt transit was detected on target, while an event was apparently ruled out in the 6" neighbor (FWHM not provided, but it looks like the stars are separated in the finder image). Rick Schwarz analyzed a full on 20210326 in 1m0 B, zs and detected ~12 min early 10-11 ppt transits in a 4.3" target aperture that excludes the majority of the flux from the 6" neighbor. On hold awaiting further interest."

name: TOI00963.01
comments_sg1: two stars on same TESS pixel; HATSouth confirms a 4 ppt event in either the target or the 4.5 neighbor. All other stars are ruled out as NEBs. TRES: The radial velocities from the three TRES observations are consistent with the photometric ephemeris for a Jupiter-mass companion. There is a dT=1.3 neighbor 4" NW, and a dT=1.1 neighbor 18" SE (but it is apparently ruled out from HATS data). Let's determine the source of the TESS detection. Pere Guerra observed a full on 20200108 in 0m4 ip. The medium size (optimized) aperture shows an apparent early event, but a large aperture around the target and 4" neighbor shows a depth consistent with zero. Pere finds a chance alignment of an event in comp star T34, but it's too far from the target. Inconclusive. SAFFIs show that the event is likely on T1 or the 4" neighbor T2. The next observation should attempt a focused full transit in a red (i', I, z) filter to to separate the 4" neighbor and determine which star the event occurs."

comments_sg1: [P=0.54771] The first TRES observation reveals a hot target star (Gaia DR2 quotes 8274 K) that is rotating too rapidly (Vrot=120 km/s) to allow useful radial velocities. V shaped; HATSouth does not detect the event on target with RMS=5 ppt in rp. No NEBs were found down to r=14. There are still a couple of stars within ~25 fainter than r=14, but still bright enough to cause the TESS detection. Felipe and the MuSCAT2 team observed a full on 20191110 in gp,rp,ip,zs. No NEBs were detected. There is a possible 40 minute early detection in the detrended data, but it occurs across a cloud break, so is not conclusive. Phil Evans observed a full on 20191129 in Rc and likely ruled out the 3 ppt event on target and all nearby stars including T2 which has dT=5.8 and is 19" NW. These observations could have missed a ~3 sigma early event. Pere Guerra observed a full on 20191208 from 1m0 zs and confirmed a 19 minute early ~3 ppt transit on target using a 4.6" uncontaminated aperture. [P= 0.547688] Pere Guerra observed a full on 20191218 in B and tentatively ruled out a 2.8 ppt V-shaped event in B. The measurement is complicated by the (apparent) variations in the target in B-band, which appear to be astrophysical .Chris S. observed a near full (terminated early by LCO) on 20200114 in 1m0 zs and detected a ~7 minute early 3 ppt event in the target aperture. We now have a zs detection, followed by a B non-detection, followed by a zs detection. The QLP data do not allow for a longer 2x, 3x, etc. period, so this system appears to show strong chromaticity. Retired as a BEB."

comments_sg1: [P=1.1980769] This TOI has a long history of tentative detections that are not consistent in timing. The field is cleared. Pere Guerra observed a defocused full on 20191230 in ip. There are two potential detections. The first is a 19 minute early event and the second is a 148 minute late (1.5-sigma) ingress at the end of the dataset. This is the 19 minute early ephemeris. [P=1.1980364] Rick Schwarz observed an (odd) full on 20200220 and detected a 16 minute early ~1.2 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 7.8 target aperture. The duration is ~15 minutes short. [P=1.1980053] Rick S. observed an (even) full on 20200227 in 1m0 ip and confirmed an on-time 1.1 ppt event on-target with the expected duration. TG Tan 20200229 Rc Inconclusive. Rick observed an (odd) full on 20200304 in ip and tentatively detected a shorter than expected roughly on-time event that showed an apparent step-up in flux mid-transit. Devin Della-Rose and the USAFA team observed a full on 20200304 in Ic and detected a ~15 minute late ~1.9 transit in a 10.2" aperture. RickS observed a near full on 2020.03.11 and detected a 1-2 ppt ~on-time transit. The depth is uncertain due to the lack of pre-OOT. The next observation should be a high precision (<1.0 ppt/3 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to check for the return of the transit at the updated ephemeris. However, note that no additional data will be included in the paper."

comments_sg1: found in faint-star QLP search; The first TRES observation reveals a composite spectrum with broad lines, so the spectroscopic parameters from the quick-look classification are unreliable. The primary peak is located on the blue edge of the correlation function, which means it is out of phase to be the source of the photometric dips. This is not worth the effort for a second TRES recon spectrum, just to see if the primary peak swaps to the red edge at phase 0.25. Steven Giacalone/LCO-CTIO-1m0 observed egresses on 20220305 and 20220307 in I and probably detected a ~20% NEB on TIC 176148949 (ΔT = 5.94, r = 9.41 N). Note that T1 in the field image is not the target star, but the apparent NEB. Retired as APC/PNEB?/SM."

name: TOI00507.01
comments: EPHEMERIS ERROR: 4 min Submitted to TFOP 25/11/19 The next observation should attempt a FULL TRANSIT ONLY red (i', I, z) filter to check for odd/even transit depth differences. If possible, also observe in a blue (U, u', B, g') filter.
comments_sg1: [P=0.8995] Phil Evans (El Sauce), Rafael Brahm (CHAT), and Pablo Lewin (Lewin Obs.) observed full transits on 2019-03-15/16/16 in Clear/ip/CBB and found depths of ~60/~55/roughly 40 ppt, respectively. The faint star combined with a relatively high airmass observation caused the Lewin data to have high scatter, so it's unclear if the apparent depth difference is a hint at chromaticity or if precision on the depth measurement is affected by the high scatter. There is a ~12 arcsec neighbor, so Phil and Pablo's ~9 apertures exclude most of the flux from the nearby star. I'm not sure of the CHAT aperture size. Howie also observed an ingress + ~ 80% on 20190321 in gp and found a short event (50% of the predicted duration), that is roughly 50 ppt deep, but there were apparent passing clouds at that time. Allyson Bieryla observed an egress + ~80% on 20190421 in ip using a 6" uncontaminated aperture and detected a ~52 ppt transit on target egressing about 10 minutes early (even orbit). Kevin Collins observed a full on 20190506 in gp and confirmed a ~58 ppt event on target (odd orbit). Will Waalkes observed a full in ip on 20190326 in ip and found a 58 ppt depth (odd orbit). [Period doubled to check even transit depth -> Px2=1.7990]. Felipe Murgas and the MuSCAT2 team observed a full on 20191120 in gp, rp, ip, zs and confirmed 15 minute early 56 to 63 ppt transits in the 5.2" target aperture that is only marginally contaminated with the nearby 9" neighbor. Based on the 1-sigma fitted depth ranges, the depths are consistent within 1-sigma. [P=0.8994547] KarenC and EricJ analyzed a full on 20210103 in MuSCAT3 gp,rp,ip,zs and detected 13 min late 54,53,56,57 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 4" target aperture. There's a mild increase in depth toward the red, but no strong chromaticity. EricJ's MCMC run favors a grazing solution with no strong evidence for chromaticity. [P=0.89946573] K. Barkaoui/SPECULOOS-S observed a full on 20230409 in gp.ip, zp and detected ~on-time (Rp/R*)^2=54, 58, 61 ppt events using an uncontaminated 2-4” target apertures. Apparent mild ~2σ chromaticity. No more SG1 observations needed at this time."

name: TOI06101.01
comments: Observed on 19 November 2023, Report: We observed an egress on 20231119 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 3.54 min early (0.7 sigma) (Rp/R*)^2: 16.3, 16.8, 16.8, 17.3 ppt event using 10.875 uncontaminated target aperture. The transit depths do not show strong chromaticity, although the significance is low due to the lack of baseline data before ingress. We can rule out all the identified nearby stars."
comments_sg1: Coel Hellier: TOI-6101.01 is a planet, WASP-149b ( https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.7761 )., but is currently not published in a journal. Dave Latham: This was identified as a transiting planet candidate by DTARPS and was assigned the name DOI-37; the first TRES observation was obtained a year ago. The two TRES observations yield a velocity offset of 146 m/s that is in phase with the DTARPS ephemeris and is consistent with a hot Jupiter companion. Retired as P/VPC/RVP, although it is not a journal published paper. Gabrielle Ross, Andrew Vanderburg, and Zoe de Beurs analyzed TIC 19342878 (T 24) from ZTF data and detected an on-target 30 ppt event using an uncontaminated 2.5 target aperture radius from ZTF and ATLAS. Jerome de Leon/ MuSCAT2 observed an egress+95% on 20231119 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time (Rp/R*)^2: 16.3, 16.8, 16.8, 17.3 ppt event using an uncontaminated 10.9" target aperture. No more SG1 pbservations needed."
publication_status_sg1: N. Schanche Confirmation In Prep. (Hellier et al. Confirmation on arxiv but not in journal)

The nearest Gaia neighbor is 12" to the SW. With a estimated FWHM of ~3", most of the flux of the neighbor is excluded from the target aperture. Tianjun observed a full transit on 20190401 in ip with nice OOT baseline and found a ~100 ppt event on target in a 6" aperture that is minimally contaminated. Howie observed a full transit on 20190329 in gp and detected a 100 ppt event in an uncontaminated 4" aperture. Kevin and Karen observed full transits in B and Zs on 20190416 and found both depths to be consistent at ~100 ppt. Felipe and the MuSCAT2 team observed a full on 20191122 in gp,rp,ip,zs and confirmed ~100 ppt transits on target, but the pipeline was apparently unable to fit independent transit depths to check for chromaticity. Retired as a VPC+."

name: AT2021zac
comments: Supernova filler. Make 15 exposure times of 60s (15 min total) per night in all channels We need 6-8 epochs over the next 40-60 days, so observe every other day. SN2021zac es una SN a redshift z=0.028. Esta SN tiene ahora magnitud g = 18 mag, pero su luminosidad está subiendo y en unos 7-10 dias alcanzará su pico de luminosidad con g ~ 16.5 mag. La SN es visible en la segunda mitad de la noche, mejor observarla al final de la noche cuando alcance airmass < 1.7.

name: TOI03794.01
comments: Teq > 2000K Observed on 23 March. Report: The scatter in the light curves is too large to allow the transit detection (the event is too shallow). We can rule out 9 stars as EBs with deep transits during the observed window.
comments_sg1: [P=2.2034138] low SNR; found in faint-star QLP search; Update to QLP s47. [P=2.2019606] RickS/KarenC/LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed an ingress (i+/-70 min; +/-4.5σ) on 20220423 in ip and apparently detected a ~30 min (2σ) late ingress-like event in a 5.3 target aperture that is contaminated with T2 ((TIC 802496350: T=18.68, dT=6.27, r=2.41" NW). T2 could not be separated, but all other stars within 2.5 arcmin were reasonably cleared of NEBs. The modeled ingress could be caused due to detrending plus the upswing at predicted ingress. A simultaneous observation from a second LCO 1-m ruled out events up to predicted ingress, but was unfortunately cut short by a camera failure. RickS/ HowieR/ KarenC/ LCO-1m0 analyzed (x4) ingresses + ~50% on 2022-11-07/16/18/27 in ip, and considering the combination of all 4 lightcurves, did not see a clear 1 ppt ingress using target apertures that are contaminated with T2 (TIC 802496350: T=18.68, dT=6.27, r=2.41" NW). Roberto Zambelli/LCO-TEID-1m0 analyzed a nominal egress+70% on 20221125 in ip and maybe detected a 121 min early ~0.9 ppt event in a contaminated 5" target aperture. Inconclusive. Karen: we have a 3rd TESS sector of this TOI from s60. I was hoping to tighten up the ephemeris and noticed that there is no QLP report from s60 (only s20 and s47 DVRs). Phill Reed observed a nominal full on 20210322 in R and detected a ~100 min early 5 ppt egress on target. Phil Evans observed an nominal ingress+80% on 20210407 in Rc and detected a ~100 min early ~5 ppt egress. [P=7.987501] Phil Evans observed an nominal ingress+80% on 20210407 in Rc and detected an apparent ~150 min early ~5 ppt egress (50 min ealerlier than Reed's apparent solid egress detection). AIJ s07,34,44,45,46 [P=7.995782, T0=1495.819] Greg Srdoc/KarenC has found that the apparent large TTVs seen in the ground-based observations are also seen in the TESS data. With a revised linear ephemeris of T0 = 1495.819, P = 7.995782 d, large (+/- ~0.1 day) E/TTVs with an apparently long super-period can be seen in the individual TESS sectors (shown below). HIRES -Yee reports a linear drift from 3 RVs, Dave Latham reports that 3 FIES+1 TRES observations show a scatter of 91 m/s that does not phase with the photometric ephemeris, and several other RV teams have collected ~5 RVs each with no further report of results. WIYN speckle imaging reports that no secondary sources were detected. SPOC shows on-target relative to know Gaia stars. Large E/TTVs. Retired as APC/VPC/RVD.

name: K2-100b
comments: Proposed by: J. de Leon and J. Livingston for atmospheres of planets in a young cluster We propose to observe a relatively unexplored population planets in young clusters. Due to their youth, these planets are experiencing high levels of UV irradiation which is the main factor of haze formation. Depending on their age, some younger planets are still undergoing contraction and hence their atmosphere if it exists are relatively puffy than current estimates. Investigating the wavelength-dependent radius variation can provide an insight to the existence such atmosphere and haze from the Rayleigh slope.

[P=1.859217]. name: TOI00727.01
comments_sg1: [P=4.7244442] SG1 to check if signal is real and if it is on the target or neighbor 149788157; period might be double; Georgia Mraz analyzed an (unintentionally defocused) full (e-144/i+267 min; -0.4/+0.7 sigma) in 1m0 zs and appears to have ruled out the 1.9 ppt event on target using a 5.5 aperture. Using an alternate reduction, including an extra aperture 180 degrees around T1 from T2 for detrending T2, all stars are cleared, except T2 can still not be cleared (there are two faint stars outside 2' that are not detected, but they are too faint and too far away to cause the TESS detection). Roberto Zambelli/ KarenC/ LCO-TEID-1m0 analyzed a (weather affected) full on 20231211 in ip and maybe detected a 5-10 m early ~1.45 ppt transit in a contaminated 4.6" target aperture, however pre-ingress baseline is minimal due to weather, and post-egress can not be flattened. The nearest star is ruled out as an NEB. The P=4.7 day 1.5 ppt event is not obvious in all sectors with AIJ analysis. Also, SPOC does not report the 4.7 d event, and QLP does not see the event in s61. AIJ and SPOC see the .02 candidate at 400 ppm at 0.75 days. However, Andrew Vanderburg confirms a ~900 ppm (deblended) 4.72 day signal in all three individual sectors (s8, 34, 61) [depth=900 ppm, T0 = 2302.1993), P = 4.7243455 (shift by 50 min earlier)]. KarenC/ LCO-SAAO-1m0 (x2) analyzed fulls of overlapping 0.01/0.02 events on 20240117 in ip and possibly detected a 0.6 ppt event consistent with both ephemerides using an uncontaminated 4.7" apertures. Guiding was poor for the lightcurve labelled -02, resulting in much higher noise. The field is cleared at these ephemerides. Rick Schwarz/ LCO-SAAO-1m0/ analyzed a full on 20241101 in ip and probably detected an ~on-time ~1.2 ppt transit using an uncontaminated 4.7" target aperture.ChrisS/ CristilynW/ LCO-Teid-1m0/ analyzed a full on 20241204 in ip and possibly detected a 0-10 min (0.5σ) early ~1 ppt event using an uncontaminated 4.3" target aperture and cleared the neighbors over the window. The interpretation of the light curve is complicated by the precision. Selcuk Yalcinkaya/TUG-T100 observed a full on 20250322 and possibly detected an ~on time ~1.3 ppt event using an uncontaminated 4.3 arcsec aperture. in-transit sky transparency took some hits due to think cloud passage. Bob Massey/LCO-TEID analyzed a defocused full 20250405 in ip and tentatively detected a 30 min (1.5σ) late 1.4 [(Rp/R*)²=1.2] ppt event using an uncontaminated 4.3" target aperture. An alternate V-shaped fit is ~1.8 ppt deep. SPOC has reported the 727.02 P=0.75 day signal, but not this 4.7 day signal. Andrew Vanderburg, QLP, and AIJ see the 4.7 day signal in s8,34,61, but the event is not as strong in the new s88. AIJ s08+s34+s61+s88 [P=4.7243555, T0=2302.1993, dur=1.6] ChrisS/ KarenC/ LCO-SSO-1m0 analyzed a full on 20250415 in ip and detected a 5 min late 1.1 event using an uncontaminated 5" target aperture. ChrisS/LCO-SAAO-1m0 analyzed a full on 20250513 in ip and detected an ~on-time ~1.5 ppt [(Rp/R*)²: 1.3 ppt] event using an uncontaminated 3.1" target aperture. No more observations needed, except for **full-only** multi-band or alternating filter high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) data in at least blue (U, u', B, g') *and* red (i', I, z, Y) filters to refine the check on chromaticity."

name: TOI00603.01
comments_sg1: [P=8.0885] Period might be double; Rick Schwarz analyzed an ODD (n-81) ingress -81/+92 min (+/-0.4σ) on 20201125 in 1m0 zs and ruled out an event on or off target over the +/-0.4σ window. Unfortunately, this is not enough coverage to rule out the 8 day period. GavinW analyzed an ODD (n=91) ingress (-220/+197 min, -0.9/+0.8σ) on 20210214 in 1m0 zs and didn't see an ingress or egress, and the field was cleared, over the observation window. Tianjun Gan analyzed an odd (n=99) egress -161/+115 mins (-0.6/+0.4 sigma) on 20210413 in 1m0 ip and probably ruled out the event on target over the window. We now have s35 TESS data. The TESS data show that no event occurred at the 8 day ephemeris in s35, but at the 2x = 16 day ephemeris, there was no s35 coverage. Thus the period is 16 days, which is consistent with a no-detect in these observations. [P=16.177] Rick Schwarz/LCO-CTIO-1m0 analyzed an ingress (-120/+141 min) on 20220505 in zs and ruled out a 2.7 ppt event over the window. The revised QLP s46 ephemeris shows the event happened after these observations ended. [P=16.1797573] Orbit confirmed. No more SG1 observations needed.

comments: EPHEMERIS ERROR: 9 min M&Ms target REPORTED by SG1 as doble-lined binary and RETIRED
comments_sg1: [P=13.5770070] either a giant planet around an M dwarf or an M dwarf binary system; Is a phantom split, but has a real dT=2.1 neighbor 1.9 ESE. Allyson observed a full on 20191219 in ip and confirmed a 12 minute late ~150 ppt in the pair of dT=1.5 target stars separated by 2". The third star between the two Gaia stars in the TICv8 is a blend of the two and is apparently not real. The brighter star companion would have a minimum (since it's V-shaped) Rc ~ 24 Re. Chris Mann observed a full on 20191219 in DragonFly gp,rp and detected 125 ppt V-shaped transits in both filters. The target aperture is contaminated with a dT=1.6 neighbor ~2" ESE. Eric Jensen and KarenC analyzed fulls on 20210102 from LCO MuSCAT3 in gp,rp,ip,zs and detected ~24 min (1.7σ) late 160,155,170,180 V-shaped transits in 0.8" target apertures that exclude a majority of the flux from the dT=2.1 neighbor 1.9" ESE. The MCMC rp/r* values are consistent within 1σ (but σ's are large), although the difference in gp and zs rp/r* is apparently more than 2σ significant. However, there is some differences in residual blending from the neighbor due to differences in the PSF size in each band 2.4,2.0,1.7,1.5", which could be causing some of the rp/r* difference. Eric's third light model shos no thrid light needed and rp/r*=0.5=~10 Re with b~1.1 grazing. We confirm this event on target, and with no strong chromaticity, but can't rule out mild chromaticity. Ian Crossfield: A HIRES spectrum shows a clear SB2. Retired as APC/VPC/SM."

name: TOI05746.01
comments: Observed on 23rd of February.2024 Report: The out-of-transit data were affected by dome vignetting, these points were removed from the transit fit. We detect a V-shaped transit event on target. The end of night report said that the weather was clear to passing clouds but the flux change seems to be to regular and steep to be caused by clouds in my opinion.
comments_sg1: [P=711.7642825] duo transit two years apart; ~711 days in the maximum period; period is likely shorter; v-shaped; The two TRES observations This is a duo system with two transits separated by 711.764 days. The velocities from three TRES observations phase up to the photometric ephemeris rather well for a period of 24.544 days, which gives exactly 29 cycles between the transits. The companion mass is probably stellar, but it depends a lot on whether this period is correct and how eccentric the orbit may be. P/29 => [P=24.5435959] Neil Thomas/ LO observed a full on 20240130 in OSC (rgb) and detected a ~9.1 min (1.23σ) early ~12.6 ppt event using a blended 40.9 target aperture (T2: TIC 318209161
depth_sg1: T=11.2, dT=0.7, r=24.1"). After accounting for the dilution from the neighbor, the actual depth is likely close to the predicted value. K. Barkaoui/TRAPPIST-North-0.6m observed a full transit on 20240223 in B filter and detected ~22 ppt V-shaped event on target using an uncontaminated 6.6” aperture. Felipe Murgas/ MuSCAT2 observed a near-full (no post-OOT) on 202402223 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected an ~on-time, V-shaped, ~20.0, 19.3, 20.7, 20.7 ppt event using a 10.9" target aperture. TRES reports an EB orbit from 9 RVs. Retired as SEB1."

name: TOI00561.03
comments: Asked by Yann Alibert for improving its transit ephemeris for CHEOPS observation. One clear transit detection is desired. No need to upload TFOP. This planet DOES NOT EXIST. Demonstrated by CHEOPS. To priority 5 as false posistive
comments_sg1: [P=16.37165] TKS detects orbit. Could be at P/2=8.185825 due to TESS data gap. Ed and the NGTS team observed an ingress on 20200305 and apparently detected an on-time ~1 ppt ingress in a 20 target aperture. RickS and KarenC analyzed an egress +/-130 min (+/- 0.5 sigma) on 20200424 in zs and apparently ruled out a 0.92 ppt event during the time covered. There is a 45 min early possible 0.3 ppt egress, but BIC prefers a linear detrend to time with no transit. Ed and the NGTS team observed an ingress on 20200305 and apparently detected an on-time ~1 ppt ingress in a 20" target aperture. [A custom SPOC run, reversing order of .01 data removal, gives P=16.28656] [Changing to P/2=8.14328 to check shorter period] Peyton Brown and KarenC analyzed an ODD (n=95) near-full on 20210321 in zs and possibly detected a 132 min (0.7σ) early ~1.3 ppt full transit on target. A 0.3 ppt 561.02 event is possibly mid-transit and could be biasing the depth a bit too deep. Delta BIC strongly prefers (>100) the detrended transit model over a detrended flat model. This event could also be consistent with the Lacedelli 25.7 day ephemeris if 4 sigma off. [P=8.142495] KarenC analyzed an EVEN (n=96) defocused nominal full on 20210330 and apparently detected a ~100 min late. The detection is not dependent on a specific set of comp stars, but the depth is dependent (0.5 - 0.8 ppt) on detrending. If the event is real, the timing would not be consistent with the Lacedelli et al 25 day period, but might be consistent with the 561.01 10.8 day period if it is 5-6 sigma late (according to the ephemeris on the SG1 TOI list). KarenC analyzed an ODD (n=97) defocused nominal near-full on 20210407 and apparently ruled out an event on target during the observation window. The two ODD and EVEN detections would suggest an 8 day period with some TTVs, but that period is apparently ruled out by TESS s35 data. [P=8.143196] AndrewV reports that the nominal 16 day ephemeris occurs in s35 TESS data gaps. The 8 day ephemeris is ruled out. RickS & KarenC/LCO-Teid-1m0 analyzed an ingress on 20211121 in zs and probably ruled out an on-time ingress. Certain comp star ensembles produce an ingress, but the depth is highly dependent on specific comp stars and is likely systematics driven. Update to Weiss et al [P=16.287] This period is apparently not correct per Gaia Lacedelli. Will be published soon."

comments_sg1: [P=10.781327+/-0.009318] Passes TRES. TKS detects orbit. Kevin and Karen observed 90% of in-transit time on 20190509 in zs, and cleared the 1.9' neighbor to the NE (T2), but not the bright neighbor to the SW (T3). Time coverage was too low and scatter too high to determine if event occured on target. Karen observed a nominal ingress+95% on 20200203 in zs and likely detected a 142 min early (0.3 sigma) 1.1 ppt egress in an uncontaminated 9.7 target aperture (defocus = 1.8 mm, FWHM=8.7"). Ed Bryant and NGTS team observed a full on 20200203 and detected a ~1.7 ppt transit in a 20" target aperture. The timing is consistent with the LCO observation. [P=10.778325] RickS observed a ingress and egress from CTIO and SSO, respectively, on 20200317 in 1m0 zs. The ingress is not distinct, but the egress is confirmed roughly on time. RobertoZ/ KarenC/ LCO-MCD-1m0 analyzed a full on 20210420 in zs. Inconclusive at the 0.9 ppt level. There is a possible event with detrending, but it is not obvious in the undetrended lightcurve. No more SG1 observations needed."

name: TOI05747.0x
comments: Target prioritario para CARMENES en cuanto lo validemos Observed on 06/11. Report: The scatter in the light curve is too large to allow the detection of the transit. We can rule out 11 stars as EBs with deep transit in the observed window. Observed on 25/11. Report: We restricted the aperture size to avoid flux contamination from a faint nearby star. The transit is too shallow to allow detection given the scatter in the light curve. We can rule out 10 stars as EBs with deep transit during the observed window. The pipeline fits a transit but the egress of this transit more or less coincides with a small jump in Y-axis, so I'm not sure if it is real. Also the fitted transit depths are larger than the prediction.

comments_sg1: [P=0.5734766] low MES; potential L1 planet; Roberto Zambelli/LCO-MCD-1m0 analyzed a full on 20221202 in ip and possibly detected on time ~1-2 ppt (depending on detrending) transit in a contaminated 12 target aperture (T2: TIC 4918921
depth_sg1: T=16.6, dT=5.8, r=9.1"). The apparent event coincides with a hit to sky transparency, so the depth is not well-constrained. Rick Schwarz/LCO-HAL-2m0M analyzed fulls on 20230119 in gp-rp-ip-zs and possibly detected 69.0 min (1.8σ) early ~0.8 to 0.9 ppt transits in uncontaminated 4.0" to 4.5" target apertures. (Rp/R*)²: 1.46 ppt (gp), 1.28 ppt (rp), 1.02 ppt (ip) & 1.02 ppt (zs). No NEBs seen, but some of the brighter stars within 2.5' are not cleared due to the expected very shallow event. SPOC prefers an event on target. [P=0.5734515] Rick Schwarz/LCO-HAL-2m0M analyzed fulls [net -5.7/+0.7σ] on 20230123 in gp-rp-ip-zs and possibly detected 32.0 min (0.8σ) [net 2.6σ] early ~0.8 to 0.9 ppt transits in uncontaminated 5.0" target apertures. (Rp/R*)²: 1.26 ppt (gp), 1.15 ppt (rp), 1.31 ppt (ip) & 1.10 ppt (zs). The zs band event is the most convincing, with the other showing consistent transit center times, but apparent much longer duration. The field is again cleared, except for T8 and T9, which are the two bright stars at ~60", and would be inconsistent with SPOC centroids. There are only three decent comp stars on the detector, and the best one is a Gaia EB at 1.79 days, which shows up as 3.898 days in TESS. Rick Schwarz/LCO-HAL-2m0M analyzed fulls (cut short@75% , so limited post egress) on 20230325 in gp-rp-ip-zs and maybe detected a 9.0 min (0.2σ) late ~1.1 to 3.1 ppt transits in uncontaminated 6.6" target apertures. (Rp/R*)²: 2.27 ppt (gp), 1.96 ppt (rp), 1.43 ppt (ip) & 3.05 ppt (zs). Substantial tracking jitter and a short post-egress OOT period limit the interpretation of the results. Rick Schwarz & Khalid Barkaoui/LCO-MuSCAT3 analyzed fulls on 20230425 in gp-rp-ip-zs and detected 2.5 min (0.06σ) early ~0.9 ppt [(Rp/R*)²=0.83, 0.84, 0.76 & 0.84 ppt] transits in uncontaminated 4.2" to 4.5" target apertures. (Rp/R*)²: 0.83, 0.84, 0.76 & 0.84 ppt. Updated period from Felipe Murgas et al. early draft combined MCMC fit [P=0.5734745]. On hold awaiting statistical validation or RV confirmation. No more SG1 observations needed."

name: TOI05139.01
comments: M&M Target OBSERVE WITH LONG 3h BASELINES BEFORE AND AFTER TRANSIT. Observed on 19 Feb. 2023 Report: The observations were affected by dome vignetting near the end of the observation, these points were not considered for the transit fit. We detect an egress on target, the egress occurred 1.7 hr earlier than the prediction. This is an M star (Teff = 3860K, V = 14.4 mag). The candidate period is of 5.94 days and the estimated radius is of 7.1 Rearth. The ephemeris of this target was already corrected once (new period), the fit was done using the latest ephemeris from SG1. Since the transit happened so earlier we may have TTVs on this one (more observations are needed).
comments_sg1: [P=5.9516865] 1st transit on momentum dump; 2nd near end of orbit; low SNR; MasonM/CarolineO/PAO observed a full (e-137/i+146 min: e-4.6σ/i+4.9σ) on 20220329 in R and ruled out the 5.4 ppt event on target with an uncontaminated aperture and cleared 0/4 neighbors over the window. AIJ analysis of s45+s46 shows the event was 150 min early, so probably egressed right before these observations started. [P=5.9465217] Update to QLP s48 [P=5.9471027] Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a nominal full on 20230219 in gp,rp,ip,zs and detected a ~102 min. early 4.37, 6.34, 4.75, 6.65 ppt event using an uncontaminated 10.87 target aperture. [P=5.9459561] Roberto Zambelli/ LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed a full on 20231208 in ip and detected an ~11 min late (0.6σ) ~5 ppt event using an uncontaminated 5.8" target aperture. [P=5.9460270]. The next observation should be a high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to check for the return of the transit at the revised ephemeris. There are no other stars within 2" from the target. 27 April 2024 update: A candidate TOI with a debris disk (50-200 Myr) pointed out by Toshiyuki Mizuki (ABC). HD 89452. Use a comparison star 5 arcmin away to the left (TYC 251-1404-1). The transit is shallow and there is no nearby star around the target and the comparison star. So make sufficient defocus and long exposures to achieve highest precision. Observed on 19/03 Report: The i-band CCD had connection issues and could not be used. The g-band photometry is 'incomplete' I think, the exposure time was set to 4s in all bands but there are fewer point in g-band than the other filters. The transit fit was done by binning the data to 60 seconds, there is some hint of a transit event in the 10 min. binned data. I'm not sure if we can call this a detection."

name: WASP-43
comments: Suggested by Nicolas Crouzet. Photometric Campaign Nov and December 2022 in coordination to JWST. * Observing parameters: - FOV: attached is a suggested FOV that includes the target (in red) and 3 comparison stars. - Defocus: the observations should be defocused, with enough defocus to avoid saturation of the bright stars. - Exposure time: previous observations used 5 s, 13 s, 10 to 30 s, 9 s for the g, r, i, z bands respectively. I do not know the amount of defocus that was used. - Guiding: using the g or r band. I suggest using the --osci option (unless things have changed since I last observed). - Duration: snapshot, ~15-20 minutes or more every night (near the end of night when the target is visible), starting now, for the next 3 weeks. Since we will be monitoring it, it would be good to keep the same parameters from night to night if possible. Don't hesitate to adjust this suggested setup.

name: SDSS-0052
comments: Real target name is SDSS J110045.14+521043.7 Collaboration with David Jones Observe a full phase curve. ON FOCUS. Short exposures on all filters 15-20 seconds First observation useless due to defocus. Go for a second Additional info: Saul, Andrew ( both in cc) and I are working with a team of amateur astronomers who have discovered an interesting short-period (96min), eclipsing white-dwarf-brown-dwarf binary. We got a beautiful g-band light curve during the NOT service night two days ago, which seems to indicate that the BD might be rather low mass, but to confirm that we really need to push our eclipse photometry into the IR to see what the contribution of the BD is. Out-of-eclipse it is nearly 19th mag in i-band, so might be a stretch for MUSCAT2 but we'd be really interested in trying. Is there any chance we'd be able to get an eclipse (or two!) observed during the next week or so? The eclipse itself only lasts around 5 minutes, so we'd only really need, say, 20 minutes total in order to get the eclipse and enough out of eclipse photometry to be useful. The binary is at 11hrs and +34deg, so we probably only have another week or two in this season to get observations before it disappears behind the Sun.

comments: M&Ms target Tianjun Gan's Project Giant planets around M dwarfs candidate. Get one more transit to check for chromaticity. Observed on 6 January 2024, Report: We observed full on 20240106 in gp, rp, ip, zs and detected a 5.48 min late (0.7 sigma) (Rp/R*)^2: 34.5, 38.2, 40.9, 43.6 ppt event using 10.875 uncontaminated target aperture. The transit depths show a hint of chromaticity. We can rule out all the identified nearby stars. Observed on May 12. Report: The observations were affected by guiding issues (large drift). We use Star ID 1 as reference since there were not bright stars outside the 2.5' area around the target. We detect a transit event on target, the transit seems to be mostly achromatic within uncertainties but this needs confirmation (the guiding issues could have affected the transit fit and the chromaticity determination). This is a M dwarf (Teff = 3526K, V = 17 mag, J = 13.4 mag). The candidate has P = 10.4 days, Rp = 8.8 Rearth so it could be giant planet or a brown dwarf. observed on 10th of May 2024 Report: We detect a transit event on the target star."

comments: TARGET CARMTESS -- Not really Here are my reports for TOI 6252.01 observed on 05/02, 07/02, 14/03, and 31/03. This is a USP candidate and a complex case because there is a bright star nearby the target and most observations do not resolve it because the data were taken with the telescope slightly defocused. ============== Report 05/02 The observations were affected by clouds and there could be some centroid problems. No significant transit detection. Report 07/02 The observations were affected by clouds. The target aperture is contaminated by Star ID 3 (TIC 307734817). There is a likely transit detection. Report 14/03 The transit window was missed according to SG1 latest ephemeris Report 31/03 The target aperture is contaminated by Star ID 1 (TIC 307734817). We detect a transit event. =============== I'm not happy with the transit fits but I've been doing the analysis for this target for too long. The transit of 07/02 appears to have a deeper depth on zs which contradicts the transit of 31/03. Of all the data sets, I would upload only 31/03 since it was made under a clearer sky.

Using 2.1 apertures that separate the 5" neighbors, the event is ruled out on the original target (T1 in the plot) and an event is maybe seen in target star (T2 in the plot) with detrending. However, with growing airmass over the observation, blending was eventually too much to get a conclusive depth in T2. K. Barkaoui/ TRAPPIST-N-0.6m observed an egress+60% on 20240205 in zp and detected a ~6 min late ~31 ppt egress using an uncontaminated 1.8” aperture. comments_sg1: [P=0.6204050] found in faint-star QLP search; We now have 10 TRES observations that yield an orbital solution for a mid M dwarf orbiting an early F dwarf with a period of 35 days. The field is very sparse, so the USP transit candidate is unlikely to be due to contamination by a nearby eclipsing binary. Ricks/KarenC LCO-1m0 on 2022-03-05/09/09/12/12/14 (6 visits) in ip and apparently detected ~1.5 ppt events that appear to come and go and/or move around. We are either detecting large TTVs or systematics that may be creating or hiding events. Date epoch odd/even phase event detected? 2022-03-05 1157 QLP-odd ~1.5 ppt at ~ -0.12 days 2022-03-09 1162 QLP-even ~1.5 ppt at ~ -0.22 days 2022-03-09 1163 QLP-odd ~2.0 ppt at ~ -0.12 days 2022-03-12 1167 QLP-odd maybe ~1 ppt @ ~ -0.14 days, or no event 2022-03-12 1168 QLP-even no obvious event from -0.2 to -0.05 days 2022-03-14 1170 QLP-even ~1.5 ppt at ~ -0.12 days 2022-03-30 ~2.0 ppt (Howie, ingress+65%) 2022-04-09 1213 QLP-even ~1.4 ppt on-time (new since joint analysis of first 6 lightcurves) Phase folding all 6 light curves shows the strongest detection of ~1 ppt @ ~ -0.12 days from epochs 1157, 1163, and 1170, but there is a hint of a dip at ~ -0.22 days from epoch 1162. The period corresponding to -0.12 days is [P = 0.620301] days. AIJ analysis of s22 + s48 FFIs shows the period is indeed P = 0.620301 +/- 0.00001 days. There is no evidence of odd/even timing offsets or depth differences, so for now, let's chaulk up the non-detections above to systematics obscuring the events or large TTVs. SPOC s48 gives a depth of 1.7 ppt. Rick Schwarz/LCO-McD-1m0 analyzed a (QLP EVEN) full on 20220409 in ip and detected an on-time ~1.4 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 3.9 target aperture. Howie Relles/LCO-TEID-1m0 analyzed an ingress + 65% on 20220330 in ip and detected an ~on-time ~2 ppt ingress in an uncontaminated 4.3" target aperture. Jerome de Leon/ MuSCAT2 observed a full on 20231202 in gp, rp, ip, zs and maybe detected a shallow transit-like signal on target, but it could be the result of guiding problems. 1 of 2 neighbor stars are ruled out as NEBs. No more SG1 observations needed at this time."

name: TOI06894.01
comments: Current Status: Priority 1 OBSERVE WITH NARROW BAND FILTERS !!!! Enric target suggestion -----Pre 20205 status----------------- Possible giant planet around an M dwarf. Transit is very deep (197.8 mmag). Use long exposures in blue bands and short-ish in red filters, suggested exposure times: g = 120s, r = 120s, i = 45s, [zs] = 15s (guiding in zs); make sure that the reference stars do not saturate (target likely will not saturate). Observations for Tianjun Gan. Observed on 19th of February. This is a giant planet candidate around M star proposed by Tianjun Gan. Report: The observations were affected by clouds. We removed g-band from the transit fit due to the large scatter seen in the light curve (target is faint in blue bands). The clouds passed by near the predicted central time of the transit, we likely detect a deep transit event. The transit seems to be deeper in r-band. Another transit observation is needed to confirm this. The fitted central time coincides with clouds passing by, I think we should try to repeat the observations with better weather. observed on 3rd of April 2024 Report: We detect a deep transit event on the target star This is a giant planet candidate around an M star. Edward Bryant is preparing a draft which includes MuSCAT2 data, I'll send these light curves to him.

name: TOI05634.01
comments: Giant planet around an M dwarf. Previously assigned to Jerome but the data was sent to Tianjun. M&Ms target Observed on May 10 Report: The target aperture is contaminated by the nearby star ID 16 (TIC 903545876). The g-band photometry presented a large scatter and it was not considered for the transit fit (target is too faint in blue bands). We detect a transit event, the transit is achromatic within uncertainties. This star is V = 16.8 mag and the observations seem to have been made with the telescope defocused. As a reminder: for faint targets like this one is necessary to focus the telescope before the observations, specially if they have stars nearby. This is a M dwarf with Teff = 3770K (J = 13.7 mag), the candidate has P = 2.2 days and R = 9.6 Rearth so it could be a gas giant around a M star (or brown dwarf). Observed on 16th of February 2024 Report: We detect an achromatic (within uncertainties) transit event using an aperture that is contaminated by a nearby star (TIC 903545876, dT=2.25, r=3.81).

name: TOI01803.01
comments: Judith's TTV target (updated 2025-08). Old comments ------------------------ Proposed by Judith Korth. We want to monitor the system for TTVs. Part of interesting multi-planet system; SG1 priority:1 I analysed the TOI 1803.02 obs. of 14/03/2021. The data is useless due to clouds and dome vignetting, I don't recommend this data set for TFOP. First observation dubious due to clouds, but promising. Let's get a second one. 25/5/2020 submitted to TFOP Report: The data is quite noisy due to clouds and bad seeing, there is a non-significant hint of a chromatic transit on target. However due to the noisy data we find this observation inconclusive. We definitely clear the field of NEB, although not all the FOV is covered. Let's observe it one more time. Observations on 16/12/2021 Report: Y The transit is not detected with significance during the observed window. The observations could be useful to TFOP to improve the ephemeris.
comments_sg1: [P=12.891153] likely multi; additional transit events at 1917 and 1924; David Baker observed a full on 20210201 in Ic and detected a ~165 min (1.6σ) early 3.5 ppt transit on target with a 7.2 uncontaminated aperture. [P=12.886746] Pere Guerra and Pau Bosch observed a full on 20210226 in OAA Ic and detected a ~3 ppt event arriving ~36 minutes (0.34σ) early in a 8.6" uncontaminated target aperture. [P=12.885828] Allyson Bieryla observed fulls on 20210528 in KeplerCam B, ip and did not detect clear 3.5 ppt events at ephemeris corresponding to two fairly clear detections back in Feb 2021. There are possible ~90 min early egresses (at different times), but they can also be detrended away with airmass. PereG/OAA observed a full on 20220127 in Ic and likely detected a ~70 min (a total of ~2σ) early 3.5 ppt transit in an uncontaminated 14.4" target aperture. The timing appears to keep changing. I'm not sure if we are seeing large TTVs or systematics. Kim McLeod and students/Whitin observed a full on 20230411 in R and detected a ~10 min (0.4σ) late ~3.4 ppt event using an uncontaminated 6.5" target aperture. Rick Schwarz/LCO-HAL-2m0M analyzed defocused (4.0) fulls on 20220415 in gp-rp-ip-zs and detected 68 min (0.3) early ~3.5 ppt transits, although the ip is shallower but could be affected by systematics. [P=12.885963] This ephem confirmed with AIJ+s49. K. Barkaoui/LCO-Teid-1.0m observed a full transit on 20220523 in gp,ip and detected 3-4 ppt events using an uncontaminated 4.7"-5.4" apertures. Nicholas Dowling/ WST observed a full on 20230506 in rp and detected an ~on-time 2.9 ppt event using an uncontaminated 3.7" target aperture. Update to Zingales+ (~15 min change)[P=12.8857790] Continue to monitor possible TTVs. Discovery paper in prep. Additional data may not be included in the initial paper."

Ashley Chontos: HI-RES/TKS collected 15 PRVs but were not able to recover an orbit that phased up with the TESS ephemeris or period. Louise Dyregaard Nielsen: HARPS RVs show little RV variation, not in phase with photometric ephemeris. RobWitt: No MINERVA-A convincing orbit detected. Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a slightly defocused full on 20230506 in gp,rp,ip,zs and detected an ~on-time ~22.2, 20.8, 19.7, 15.9 ppt event using a contaminated 10.87" target aperture. (TIC 950525830 T = 14.3 ΔT = 1.9 r = 2.2"). HowieR/ KarenC/ LCO-HAL-0m4 analyzed full on 20230424 in ip and detected a 21 ppt event in a 6.9" target aperture that is contaminated with TIC 950525830 (T = 14.3 ΔT = 1.9 r = 2.3" W). A smaller 1.7" aperture shows the 20 ppt event apparently on-target, but the telescope goes out of focus throughout the time-series, so the results are not conclusive. FabianF/ RFAC observed a full on 20240413 in Red and detected a ~6 min late, ~10 min longer duration, ~19.8 ppt event using a contaminated 5.9" target aperture (T2: dT=1.9, r=2.3" W). The T1/T2 pair could not be separated. K. Barkaoui/ TRAPPIST-N-0.6m observed an egress+50% on 20240521 in I+z' and detected an ~on-time ~15.5 ppt egress using a contaminated 6.6” target aperture. K. Barkaoui/ KarenC/ LCO-Teid-1.0m analyzed a full on 20250426 in gp,ip and detected blended 15, 16.7 ppt events using contaminated 7", 6" apertures blended with T2 (TIC 950525830, T = 14.302, ΔT = 1.90, r = 2.2") at 2.2". Seeing was poor, but using a 1.1" target aperture that excludes probably 70% of the flux from T2, the event is possibly still there with double detrending. name: TOI03884.01
comments: This object was introduced as a validation target for M2. Planet around M star. Observed on 21/12. Report: The i-band CCD had connection issues and could not be used. We removed g-band data in the transit fit due to its large scatter. The transit is not detected on target during the observed window. We can ruled out EBs with deep transit for 3 stars. The pipeline fits a transit-like event, more visible in zs-band, but this is supposed to be a 32.5 mmag transit event so I don't think this transit is real. Submitted to TFOP on 10 January 2022 We'll get an updated ephemeris from s46. Observations on 4/March 2022 Bad due to weather Observed on April 21st. Report: We detect an egress on target, the egress seems to be chromatic.

name: TOI05526.01
comments: Added by F. Murgas. Possible new KESPRINT Target. Period of 21.7 days, only 1 transit visible from MuSCAT2 when proposed by KESPRINT for follow-up. Observed on 18/05. Report: The transit event is too shallow to be detected given the scatter in the light curve. We can rule out 2 stars as EBs in the observed window. This is a potential KESPRINT target with a period of 21.7 days; although the star is a little faint for RVs (V = 11.3 mag, it is an M star). The photometry has a large scatter because the reference stars are faint, Star id 1 was the brightest one but it is variable. Also there was some vignetiting at the end of the observations (with airmass ~2.0) that I left out of the transit fit.
comments_sg1: [P=21.7300248, T0=2283.8666001] Low SNR; possible odd-even; period could be half (~10.87 days); Felipe Murgas/MuSCAT2 observed a nominal full on 20220518 in gp,rp,ip,zs and cleared 2 out of 119 neighbours over the window, did not see an obvious NEB. Rick Schwarz/KarenC/LCO-Teid-1m0 analyzed a full (e-211/i+211; -30/+30σ) on 20220518 in ip and ruled out a 1.1 ppt event on target, unless there's a short V-shaped event near mid-transit. There a possible >>5σ early 300 ppt event in T4 (TIC 952531020: T=18.37, dT=7.55, r=41.9 WSW), but it is near another star, so could be due to the large change in FWHM during roughly the same time. Also, SPOC centroids strongly suggest the event is on-target. The published 21.7 day period is twice that found by SPOC since the even signal is much stronger than the odd signal. However, the published T0 corresponds to the SPOC odd events, so we need to add 21.7/2 days to T0 to check the even phase. These data apparently rule out the original 10.9 day period. Rick Schwarz/LCO-SSO-1m0 analyzed an ingress (33.3% - i+52
depth_sg1: +5.8σ) on 20230214 in ip and possibly detected a 21 min (2.3σ) late 3.1 ppt event using an uncontaminated 7.0" target aperture. [T0=2294.7316125] Rick Schwarz/LCO-CTIO-1m0 analyzed a full on 20230308 in ip and possibly detected an 11.0 min (1.1σ) early 1.3 ppt transit in the detrended lightcurve using a 6.2" target aperture. If the event is real, these data support the T0_new = T0+P, and P_new=2xP, where P=10.87 day. [P=21.7297936] Rick Schwarz/LCO-Teid-1m0 analyzed fulls (x2) on 20230329 in ip and possibly detected a 23.0 min (2.3σ) early 1.7 ppt transit using a 7.0" target aperture. Despite best efforts, I don't think the results are reliable. There are just no good comp stars in the LCO 1m FOV. We've need instruments with even larger fields of view. Rick Schwarz/ CristilynW/ LCO-SSO-0m35 analyzed a full on 20240311 in ip and maybe detected an ~on-time ~1.2 ppt event using an uncontaminated 5.8" target aperture. The timing jumps around with different detrending/comp ensemble. The data are not sensitive at 1 ppt and there is lack of good comp stars in the FOV. Rick Schwarz/ CristilynW/ LCO-CTIO-1m0 analyzed a weather-affected full on 20240424 in zs and possibly detected a ~10-20 min early ~1.3 ppt event using an uncontaminated 5.8" target aperture. [P=21.7295230]. Howie Relles/ CristilynW/ LCO-SAAO-1m0/ analyzed a weather-affected full on 20240515 in zs and, depending on comp star selection, possibly detected a ~10-20 min late ~1 ppt event using an uncontaminated 5.8" target aperture. No more observations needed, except for extreme precision (<0.5 ppt/10 min) full transits from detectors with large FOVs (>1'x1') to check for the the shallow event on target in a red (r', R, i', I, z, Y) filter."

name: TOI05708.01
comments: Observed on June 17 2023: Report: The observations were made with the telescope defocused (target is bright) and the target aperture is contaminated with the flux of a nearby star (Star ID 2). We do not detect the transit event on target although the photometry is affected by flux of the nearby star. We can rule out 3 stars as EBs with deep transits in the observed window. Felipe: I was the observer for this one, there weren't good targets for that first half of the night and the star was bright so the telescope needed to be defocused. I tried a smaller aperture for the fit trying to exclude the nearby star but the photometry had a large scatter.
comments_sg1: V-shaped; centroids slightly closer to TIC 335813326; Check field within 2.5' for NEBs using a red (rp, R, ip, I, z) filter. The next observation should be a focused high precision (<1.0 ppt/10 min) full transit in a red (r', R, i', I, z) filter to determine the source and timing of the event using an aperture that excludes most of the flux from the 6 neighbor."

name: TOI05628.01
comments: Hot Jupiter around an M3 star, paper in preparation for Tianjun Gan. We need a transit to constain the ephemeris since last observation was 1 year go. Check transit times with TESS Transit Finder (TTF), there is a 1.5 hour diference between the two, chose a big observing window. Observe it with long exposures in blue bands (g = 60s, r = 60s at least). observed on 31st of March 2024 Report: We do not detect the transit event with significance, scatter in the light curve is comparable to the transit depth (transit is shallow). The observations were made with the telescope defocused, the target aperture is contaminated by the flux of a nearby star (Star ID 11, TIC 1270806721). We can rule out 7 stars as EBs with deep transits during the observed window.
comments_sg1: [P=4.3415097] [original depth = 104 ppt] [The similar mag neighbor in TIC is an unmatched tmgaia2 and cooldwarfs duplicate entry. Confirmed by Martin Paegert.] [Cooldwarf gives R*= 0.296 Rsun.] host is likely an M dwarf which would allow Rp to be planetary; K. Barkaoui/LCO-Teid-1m0 analyzed a full on 20220609 in zs and detected an ~on-time ~50 ppt (instead of 104 ppt) event using an uncontaminated 3.9 target aperture. TICv8 shows an apparent ~4" E similar magnitude neighbor, but TIC reports the target star as from tmgaia2, and the neighbor as being from cooldwarfs, so are duplicates in the TICv8. This would explain the ground-based depth of ~50 ppt instead of 104 ppt deblended depth reported by SPOC. Gaia also does not see the neighbor. Cooldrwarfs reports R*=0.296+/-0.05 Rsun. With a depth of 50 ppt, the companion would have radius Rp = 7 Re. Howie Relles/LCO-SAAO-1m0 analyzed an ingress + 65% on 20230125 in rp and detected a 14.2 min. late 50 ppt ingress in an uncontaminated 3.9" target aperture. K. On hold awaiting statistical validation or RV confirmation. No more SG1 observations needed at this time."

comments: Angelica Psaridi Project: change priority from 5 to 1 (24 Oct 2025). The idea is to improve the radius estimate using TESS + MuSCAT2. M star: CARMENES or M&Ms candidate Confirmed from the ground on target- Observed de-focussed if necessary. I've done the photometry and analysis for the observations of TOI 2068.01 from 27.12.2020. The data is useless because of dome vignetting. Observation on 24/05/2022 bad due to weather. Let0s go for a third transit Observed on July 2nd 2023 Report: We did not use g- and r-band photometry due to their lack of points, possible there were issues with the astrometry. The light curve scatter is to high to allow the transit detection (the transit event is too shallow). We can rule out 2 stars as EBs with deep transits in the observed window. Let's try one more time Paper in prep. by Priyashkumar Mistry and VaTEST Team. Observed on 20th of February 2024 Report: The brightest reference star (Star ID 1) was saturated in g and r filters. Using the fainter reference stars (Star IDs 3,4,5) we do not detect the transit due to the large scatter in the light curve. By using Star ID 1 as reference star, we detect the transit event in i and zs bands. For the observers: if there is only 1 bright star to be used as reference, please check that it doesn't saturate in all the bands. I think we should upload to ExoFOP only the i and zs light curves.
